---
title: "1.15 String Manipulation"
subtitle: "Part 3: String Index and Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit1-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## String Index and Length

### Positions start at 0

A string holds characters in a sequence.

Each character is at a position or **index** which starts with `0` as shown below.

An **index** is a number associated with a position in a string.

The length of a string is the number of characters in it including any spaces or special characters.

The string below has a length of `14`.

## String Index Figure

### Source figure

![A string with the position index shown above each character](../../assets/1-using-objects-and-methods/1-15-string-manipulation/stringIndicies.png){fig-align="center" width="68%"}

## Source Note

### Valid index range

The first character in a string is at index `0`.

The last character is at `length - 1`.

Attempting to access indices outside this range will result in an **IndexOutOfBoundsException**.

## String Methods

### AP CSA exam methods

The `String` class includes many methods to process strings.

For the AP CSA exam, you only need to know how to use the following `String` methods.

Their descriptions are included in the AP CSA Java Quick Reference Sheet that you get during the exam, so you don't have to memorize these.

## String Methods

### Reference methods

| Method | Source description |
|---|---|
| `int length()` | returns the number of characters in the string, including spaces and special characters like punctuation |
| `String substring(int from, int to)` | returns a new string starting at `from` and ending at the character before `to` |
| `int indexOf(String str)` | searches for `str` and returns the beginning index or `-1` if not found |
| `int compareTo(String other)` | returns negative, `0`, or positive based on alphabetical order |
| `boolean equals(String other)` | returns true when the characters are the same |

## `length`, `substring`, `indexOf`

### Source explanation

Run the code below to see the output from the `String` methods `length`, `substring`, and `indexOf`.

The `length` method returns the number of characters in the string, not the last index, which is `length - 1`.

The `str.substring(from,to)` method returns the substring from the `from` index up to, but not including, the `to` index.

The method `str.indexOf(substring)` searches for the substring in `str` and returns the index of where it finds substring in `str` or `-1` if it is not there.

## Code Example

### `activecode:: lcsm1`

This code shows the output from `String` methods `length`, `substring`, and `indexOf`.

How many letters does `substring(0,3)` return?

What does `indexOf` return when its argument is not found?

## Starter Code

### `lcsm1`

::: {.code-scroll .compact}
```java
public class Test1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String message1 = "This is a test";
        String message2 = "Hello Class";
        // TODO: trace each String method output before running.

        System.out.println(message1.length());
        System.out.println(message2.length());

        System.out.println(message1.substring(0, 3));
        System.out.println(message1.substring(2, 3));
        System.out.println(message1.substring(5));

        System.out.println(
                message1.indexOf("is")); // This will match the is in "This"!
        System.out.println(message1.indexOf("Hello"));
        System.out.println(message2.indexOf("Hello"));

        // lowercase and uppercase are not on the AP exam, but still useful
        System.out.println(message2.toLowerCase());
        System.out.println(message2.toUpperCase());
    }
}
```
:::

## Expected Output

### `lcsm1`

```text
14
11
Thi
i
is a test
2
-1
0
hello class
HELLO CLASS
```

`substring(0,3)` returns `3` letters.

`indexOf` returns `-1` when its argument is not found.

## Source Note

### Single-character substring

Remember that `substring(from,to)` does not include the character at the `to` index.

To return a single character at index `i`, use:

```java
str.substring(i, i + 1)
```

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: substring-preconditions`

The following code breaks the preconditions of the `substring` method and throws an `IndexOutOfBoundsException`.

Can you fix the code by changing the arguments for the `substring` method to print out the substring `"o"`?

## Broken Starter Code

### `substring-preconditions`

```java
public class SubstringPreconditions
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String str = "hello";
        // TODO: fix the substring bounds to print "o"
        System.out.println(str.substring(-1, 10));
    }
}
```

## Test Requirement

### `substring-preconditions`

Runestone checks that `main` prints:

```text
o
```

The valid call for `"o"` uses the index of the last character and stops before the next index.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qsb_3`

What is the value of `pos` after the following code executes?

```java
String s1 = "abccba";
int pos = s1.indexOf("b");
```

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `2`
- B. `1`
- C. `4`
- D. `-1`
:::

Correct answer: B. `1`

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qsb_3b`

What is the value of `len` after the following code executes?

```java
String s1 = "baby";
int len = s1.length();
```

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `2`
- B. `3`
- C. `4`
- D. `-1`
:::

Correct answer: C. `4`

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qsb_3c`

What is the value of `s2` after the following code executes?

```java
String s1 = "baby";
String s2 = s1.substring(0,3);
```

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `baby`
- B. `b`
- C. `ba`
- D. `bab`
:::

Correct answer: D. `bab`

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qsb_4b`

What is the value of `s2` after the following code executes?

```java
String s1 = "baby";
String s2 = s1.substring(2);
```

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `by`
- B. `aby`
- C. `a`
- D. `b`
- E. `ba`
:::

Correct answer: A. `by`

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- identify the first index as `0`
- identify the last valid index as `length() - 1`
- use `length()` for the number of characters
- trace `substring(from, to)` as stopping before `to`
- use `substring(i, i + 1)` for one character
- use `indexOf` returning `-1` when the target is not found
:::

## End

### 1.15 Part 3 complete

Part 4 continues with `compareTo`, `equals`, and common mistakes.
